content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural delay of the selection age (Finnish/Polish model)'. International difference-in-differences evidence indicates that delaying selection from age 10–12 to age 14 would likely reduce Hungary's socioeconomic achievement gap by approximately 0.15–0.25 SD (based on Polish comparable gains), but this would require compensatory instructional resources and teacher supply that Hungary currently lacks in demonstrable quantity.
opportunities: Potential achievement gains in the range of Poland's observed 0.16-0.28 SD across subjects, with the largest gains for at-risk/vocational-track-bound students, if Hungary's context proves sufficiently comparable.

Reduced intergenerational income elasticity, by analogy with Finland's observed 23% reduction (0.30 to 0.23) following its delay of tracking from 11 to 16, though Finland's effect was bundled with welfare-state expansion and teacher-education reform not isolated from the tracking-age effect itself.
strengths: Difference-in-differences evidence from 75 countries/regions over 20 years shows early tracking increases test-score dispersion across PISA, PIRLS, and TIMSS cycles, though effect sizes vary substantially by study pair.

Early-tracking countries show larger inequality increases between primary and secondary school than late-tracking countries; Germany (+0.71), Greece (+0.30) and Czech Republic (+0.25) contrast with Turkey (−0.63), New Zealand (−0.50), Canada (−0.32).
threats: The scenario identifies a substantial reversal risk citing the Polish precedent (reform reversed after 18 years despite documented gains), and the only entrenchment device contemplated (a cross-party political pact) is not specified with statutory safeguards, independent monitoring triggers, or supermajority requirements, leaving the reform structurally exposed in the same way Poland's was.

Fidesz's immediate framing of any 'unified model' as totalitarian ('no example since communism since') establishes this scenario as the highest-visibility target for culture-war opposition among all five, given it most closely matches the minister's initially floated (then retracted) unified four-year model idea.
weaknesses: Whether the Polish 1999 delayed-tracking reform's effect sizes (0.25–0.28 SD) would replicate in Hungary given different demographic composition (Roma representation, migration patterns) and school-system institutional differences.

The causal attribution of Hungary's 53-point socioeconomic achievement gap to early selection versus other structural factors (catchment segregation, resource allocation within schools, teacher quality distribution, out-of-school learning inequality).
